segments.s345-g1: همی بود is a classical/perfective-continuous past construction: همی (continuous/ever) + بود (past of 'to be'), so 'was continually' or 'kept being'. گشتاسپ is the proper name (subject). دل مستمند is a nominal predicate: دل 'heart' followed by the adjective/participle مستمند 'needy/afflicted', so 'Gushtasp's heart was needy/longing.'
segments.s345-g2: This continues describing the same heart: خروشان و جوشان are two coordinated participial/adjectival forms describing violent motion or agitation ('roaring' and 'seething'). ز + چرخ بلند is an ablative phrase 'from/of the lofty sky/heaven' (چرخ as sky/heaven, بلند 'lofty'), giving the cause or source of that agitation.
segments.s345-g3: نیامد is the simple past negative 'did not come' (or 'there came not'). ز گیتیش is 'from his world' (گیتی 'world' with the enclitic -ش 'his'). جز introduces an exception: 'except'. زهر 'poison' is the only thing mentioned as coming; بهر here functions as 'for/unto/therefore' linking to what follows (classical use can mark purpose or relation).
segments.s345-g4: یکی is an indefinite 'a certain' (someone/one). روستا 'village' is the object of دید 'saw' (past). نزدیک شهر is a prepositional/adverbial phrase 'near the city' (نزدیک before شهر). Word order is typical Persian: determiner + noun + verb, with locative phrase after the verb.
segments.s345-g5: A simple coordinate list: درخت و گل و آبهای روان = 'tree and flower and the running waters.' آبهای is the plural (آب + های) 'waters', and روان is an adjective/epithet 'flowing/running', placed after the noun as in classical Persian.
segments.s345-g6: نشستنگه is a compound noun 'place to sit / seat / sitting-place'; شاد 'joyful' qualifies that place. مَرد جوان 'the young man' follows and is the possessor/beneficiary (i.e. 'the young man's joyful seat' or 'a joyful seat for the young man'). Classical Persian often omits an explicit ezāfe (ِ) in written epic lines, so juxtaposition expresses the genitive relation.
segments.s345-g7: درختی is an indefinite 'a tree' (the -ی ending can mark indefiniteness). گشن here is an epithet attached to the tree (classical/poetic adjective), and سایه بر پیش آب is a prepositional phrase 'shade upon/before the water' (بر پیش = 'in front of / before'). The order places the attributes before the locative phrase for compact poetic description.
segments.s345-g8: نهان گشته is a compound perfect/participial clause: نهان 'hidden' + گشته (perfect participle of شدن) = 'has become hidden / was hidden'. زو 'from it' (z + او) gives the source (referring back to the water), and چشمه آفتاب 'the spring of the sun' (a poetic phrase for sunlight/radiance) is the thing hidden. Word order places the participle phrase before the source and the noun-phrase object.
segments.s345-g33: چو is a temporal conjunction meaning 'when'. آن مهتر is the noun phrase 'that elder/chief' (demonstrative + noun) and آمد is the simple past 'came'. سوی is a preposition 'toward / to' and خان خویش is a possessive phrase 'his khan/house' where خوی + the enclitic ش together form the possessive 'خویش' ('his'). Word order is Subject — Verb — directional phrase (آن مهتر آمد سوی خان خویش = 'that elder came toward his khan').
segments.s345-g34: به مهمان is a prepositional phrase 'for/to the guest' placed before the verb. بیاراست is an archaic/poetic past/preterite-form of آراستن meaning 'he adorned / prepared'. ایوان خویش ('his iwan') is the direct object phrase, again formed from خوی + enclitic ش = خویش 'his'. The verb connects back to the subject of the previous clause (he prepared his iwan for the guest).
segments.s345-g35: بسان is a preposition 'like/as', so بسان برادر means 'as a brother'. همی is a habitual/continuous particle (common in epic Persian) that gives a continuing or customary aspect. داشتش is the past-tense verb داشت ('kept/held/treated') with the enclitic object ش 'him' — together 'he treated/kept him as a brother'.
segments.s345-g36: زمانی here is adverbial meaning 'at times/when necessary' (temporal). به ناکام is a prepositional phrase meaning roughly 'into / in a state of defeat/failure' and نگذاشتش is the negative past 'did not let / did not leave' with enclitic ش = 'him' (so 'he did not leave him unsuccessful/defeated'). The clause modifies/continues the previous idea of how he treated the man.
segments.s345-g37: زمانه is a noun meaning 'the times / fortune'; برین is a fused form (بر + ین) meaning 'on/with this' or 'thus' (older spelling of برِ این). نیز is 'also', چندی is an adverb 'for a while', and بگشت is a past verb 'turned / changed' (poetic past). The clause says that the state of affairs/fortune also changed for a while.
segments.s345-g38: برین کار is 'this work/affair' (بر + ین = 'this'), بر ماهیان is a prepositional phrase 'to/onto the fishes' (بر + ماهیان), and برگذشت is the past verb 'passed over / was transferred' (i.e. the affair passed on). Together the clause means 'this affair passed on to the fishes' — a literal phrasing preserved from the poem.
segments.s345-g25: This is a poetic, inverted word order: بدو ('to him') comes first, then گفت (said) and finally the subject گشتاسپ (Gushtasp). کای is a vocative particle 'O' addressing the addressee, and نامجوی is an epithet 'name-seeker / seeker of fame' modifying the person spoken to. Together the clause means 'Gushtasp said to him, "O seeker of fame..."'.
segments.s345-g26: نژاد تو از کیست is a question 'your lineage from whom is?' — از کیست is the classical way to ask 'of/from whom are you (descended)?' ست is the copula 'is'. با من بگوی literally 'speak with me' functions as an imperative/request 'tell me' (بگوی here is a polite/formal classical form). Word order places the question before the request.
segments.s345-g27: چنین داد پاسخ is literal 'thus gave an answer' — the verb داد (gave) follows the adverb چنین and the noun پاسخ (answer). ورا is the dative pronoun 'to him', and کدخدای (the headman) appears at the end as the agent; this postposed subject is typical of epic/poetic word order.
segments.s345-g28: کزین پرسش اکنون ترا چیست رای is a compact question: کزین 'from this / about this', پرسش 'question', اکنون 'now', ترا (written here as ت + را) = 'to you' (accusative/dative), and چیست رای = 'what is (your) opinion/counsel?'. Note the noun رای ('opinion') comes at the end; چی(ست) is 'what is'.
segments.s345-g29: من از تخم شاه آفریدون is a straightforward genitive phrase 'I (am) from the seed of King Afrīdūn.' گرد is an archaic/poetic element here attached at the end of the clause; in epic Persian گرد can function like a verbal/copulative element (roughly 'was/became/come from') rather than the common meanings listed in the lemma.
segments.s345-g30: کزان تخمه 'from whose offspring' is a relative phrase referring back to آفریدون. The clause کس در جهان نیست خرد uses a negative existential construction: کس 'anyone', در جهان 'in the world', نیست 'does not exist', and خرد ('wisdom') is the quality implied — the poet means 'there is no one in the world of (equal) wisdom from that stock' (i.e. none wiser). The word order places the negative verb before the quality for emphasis.
segments.s345-g31: چو بشنید 'when he heard' uses چو + past tense بشنید (he heard) to form a temporal clause. گشتاسپ is the subject, and برداشت پای literally 'took up (his) foot' — an idiom meaning 'he rose' or 'set out.' برداشت here is a past action 'he lifted/removed' applied to پای (foot).
segments.s345-g32: همی رفت is the classical periphrastic past/progressive: همی (a preverb/particle used in older Persian) + past رفت 'went' = 'he went / set off.' با نامور کدخدای 'with the famed headman' is a prepositional phrase attached to the verb indicating accompaniment; نامور is an epithet 'renowned/famed'.
segments.s345-g17: ورا دید is an archaic/object‑before‑verb order: ورا = 'to him' (object/indirect object) followed by دید 'he saw' (simple past). با دیدگان پر ز خون is an instrumental/qualifying phrase 'with eyes full of blood' where با 'with' precedes the noun phrase; پر ز is poetic for پر از 'full of'.
segments.s345-g18: This clause is a participial/ verbless descriptive phrase attached to the sight: به زیر + noun phrase indicates position 'under/beneath'; دست کرده is a compound of دست 'hand' + کرده (past participle) meaning 'having placed/with (his) hand placed'; ستون here describes the posture metaphorically as 'a pillar' or 'as a support'. Word order is prepositional phrase -> object -> participle -> predicate noun.
segments.s345-g19: بدو گفت is again object-before-verb: بدو 'to him' (dative/enclitic-like pronoun) + گفت 'said' (narrative past). کای is the vocative particle 'O' addressing someone; پاک modifies the following noun phrase مرد جوان 'young man' (پاک + [مرد جوان]). The sequence is vocative → adjectives → noun.
segments.s345-g20: چرایی functions as an interrogative noun 'what is the reason / why'. پر از درد is the idiom 'full of pain' (پر + از), where از is the partitive/prepositional marker. و connects the second predicate تیره‌روان, a compound adjective meaning 'dark‑souled' or 'gloomy', completing the question about his state.
segments.s345-g21: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'. آیدت is the impersonal/occurrence verb آید 'it comes' with the 2nd‑person enclitic -ت 'to you' (literally 'comes to you'). رای is the noun 'thought/plan' that is the thing that might occur, and ایوان من is the possessed NP 'my iwan/porch'.
segments.s345-g22: This short noun phrase names what is desired if the condition holds: بوی شاد is literal 'the scent/smell of joy' used metaphorically (brought cheer), یکچند can mean 'for a while' or 'a few'—here it qualifies مهمان 'guests' as a small number or brief visit, and من is the possessor/beneficiary 'my'. The verb (implied) is elliptical—classical Persian often omits the verb in such hortatory lines.
segments.s345-g23: مگر here has the sense 'unless' (introducing the negative/exceptional condition). کین غمان is a genitive/compound 'this/that grief of hatred' (کین = grudge/hatred, غمان = sorrows). بر دلت is 'upon your heart' (بر = on/upon, دل + -ت second‑person suffix), and کم شود is the passive/become construction 'become less' (کم 'little' + شود 'becomes').
segments.s345-g24: This is a poetic image: سر تیر = 'the head/point of the arrow', مژگانت = 'your eyelashes' (here eyelashes are likened to arrows), بی نم = 'without moisture/tears' (بی = without, نم = tear/dew), and شود 'become'. The whole is a wish/blessing that the points of your lash‑arrows not become dry of tears.
segments.s345-g9: بران سایه بنشست مرد جوان is a locative phrase + verb + subject in classical word order: 'بران سایه' (= 'on/under that shade') is the locative prepositional phrase placed before the verb; بنشست is the 3rd-person singular past of نشستن 'to sit (down)'; مرد جوان is the noun phrase 'the young man' and appears after the verb (typical of Ferdowsi's poetic inversion).
segments.s345-g10: پر از درد پیچان و تیره‌روان is a adjectival/participial phrase modifying the young man: پرِ + از = the fixed collocation 'پر از' meaning 'full of'; درد پیچان is a compound where پیچان (twisting/convulsive) qualifies درد (pain); و links the second quality تیره‌روان, an adjectival compound meaning 'gloomy/ill‑fated.'
segments.s345-g11: همی گفت کای داور کردگار: همی + past verb (گفت) gives the iterative/continuous sense 'kept saying' (a frequentative use common in epic); کای is the vocative 'O'; داور کردگار is a two‑word vocative apposition 'Judge, Creator' addressing the divine arbiter.
segments.s345-g12: غم آمد مرا بهره زین روزگار is a simple past clause: غم آمد 'sorrow came'; مرا (here written as م + را in the word list) marks the experiencer/indirect object 'to me' (older usage uses را with the clitic); بهره زین روزگار is a noun phrase 'the share/portion of this time/age' — i.e. 'my allotted lot from these times.' Word order places the coming-of‑sorrow before its recipient and then the phrase stating it as the allotted portion.
segments.s345-g13: نبینم همی اختر خویش بد: همی gives a continuous/iterative flavor; اختر خویش = 'my own star' (خویش is the possessive reflexive clitic); بد qualifies the star as 'unfavorable.' نبینم here is a poetic/formal form used by Ferdowsi in the sense 'I perceive / I find' (the negative form in modern Persian would contradict the sense, so treat it as a classical/poetic usage meaning 'I see/regard' or 'I no longer see [it as good]'). Word order puts the verb before the possessed noun phrase for emphasis.
segments.s345-g14: ندانم چرا بر سرم بد رسد: ندانم = 'I do not know'; چرا = 'why'; بر سرم (lit. 'upon my head') = 'upon me' (idiomatically 'befall me'); بد رسد uses رسد (3rd-person present/past-form used to mean 'befall/ happen') with بد 'evil/misfortune' as its subject/quality — together 'why bad befalls me.'
segments.s345-g15: یکی نامور زان پسندیده ده: یکی نامور = 'a certain famed/renowned one'; زان = 'from that' (indicating origin or group); پسندیده = 'chosen/approved.' Together the phrase identifies 'a renowned man chosen from that group.' The short, verbless stylistic clause is typical of epic introduction of a character.
segments.s345-g16: گذر کرد بر وی که او بود مه: گذر کرد is the past action 'he passed by' (گذر + کرد acting as the verbal periphrasis 'passed'); بر وی = 'by him/in front of him'; که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'for/and he was ...'; او بود مه = 'he was the moon' (مه = moon, used metaphorically for a bright/radiant person).

sentences.s345-s1: Gushtasp's heart was continually longing, tumultuous and seething from the lofty heavens.
sentences.s345-s2: From his world there came nothing but poison; for that reason he saw a certain village near the city.
sentences.s345-s3: Trees and flowers and running waters — the young man's joyful seat.
sentences.s345-s4: A tree cast its broad shade before the water; from it the spring of sunlight was hidden.
sentences.s345-s17: When that elder came to his khan, he set his iwan in order for the guest.
sentences.s345-s18: He kept him as if a brother, and whenever needed he did not leave him thwarted.
sentences.s345-s19: The fortunes, too, for a while were thus changed; this affair passed on to the fishes.
sentences.s345-s13: Gushtasp said to him, 'O seeker of fame, tell me: from whose lineage are you?'
sentences.s345-s14: The headman thus answered him: 'What is your opinion now about this question?'
sentences.s345-s15: I am of the seed of King Afrīdūn; from whose offspring there is no one in the world wiser.
sentences.s345-s16: When Gushtasp heard, he lifted his foot and went with the famed headman.
sentences.s345-s9: He saw him with eyes full of blood, his hand placed under his chin like a pillar.
sentences.s345-s10: He said to him, 'O pure young man, why are you full of pain and so dark‑souled?'
sentences.s345-s11: If the thought of my iwan comes to you, (send) a few merry guests to me.
sentences.s345-s12: Unless these sorrows of hatred lessen in your heart, may the arrow‑tips of your eyelashes not be without moisture (tears).
sentences.s345-s5: A young man sat under that shade, full of wrenching pain and a gloomy spirit.
sentences.s345-s6: He kept saying, “O Judge, Creator, sorrow has come to me as my portion of this age.”
sentences.s345-s7: I can no longer view my own star as favorable; I do not know why misfortune befalls me.
sentences.s345-s8: A certain famed man, one chosen from that company, passed by him — for he was like the moon.
